topic: Someone finds an unsigned birthday card in their mailbox
raw_output:
[story 1]

The mail slot clattered open, and there it was: a thick, cream-colored card, completely blank save for a single, hastily scrawled word at the bottom—"Mine." My hand froze mid-reach, the paper trembling slightly in my grip. I pulled it out, the edges crisp under my fingertips. My breath hitched, a sharp, shallow intake that stole the air from my lungs. I stared at the word, then around the quiet hallway, suddenly feeling exposed, as if some unseen eye had just cataloged my secret. A cold prickle crawled up my spine, and I quickly shoved the card deep into my pocket, my movements jerky and unnatural. I had to get away from it.

[story 2]

I picked up the envelope, expecting a cheerful greeting, but the card inside was unsettling. It was pristine white, folded with meticulous precision, and the only inscription was a date, written in elegant, looping script, followed by a single, stark numeral. My knuckles turned white as I held it. A wave of nausea washed over me, making the room seem to tilt. I dropped it instantly, scrambling backward until my back hit the cool plaster of the wall. My heart was hammering against my ribs, a frantic, trapped thing. I couldn't look at it again; the sheer wrongness of the gesture made my stomach clench painfully. I needed to burn it, to erase the evidence of that silent, strange communication.

[story 3]

The cardboard slipped from the mailbox, landing softly on the welcome mat. It was a birthday card, undeniably, but the message inside was utterly vacant. Just a single, drawn-out line of text that seemed to vibrate with an alien intent. I knelt down, my knees protesting the sudden movement, and peered closer. My eyes scanned the paper, searching for any clue, any sign of context, but there was nothing but the stark, unadorned script. A tremor ran through my hands, making the paper flutter dangerously. I snatched it up, my entire body tensing, every muscle coiled tight. I stood up abruptly, backing away slowly, the silence of the apartment suddenly deafening, as if the card itself was emitting a low, menacing hum.
